# Izvori i reference

Svi izvori korišteni u razvoju ovog MMA programa treninga, organizovani po kategorijama.

---

## 1. Metodologije trenera i knjige

1. **Joel Jamieson** — *Ultimate MMA Conditioning*, 2009. Web stranica: [8weeksout.com](https://www.8weeksout.com). Bioforce HRV sistem i pristup energetskim sistemima za MMA kondiciju. Intervjui i članci dostupni na 8weeksout.com/blog.

2. **Phil Daru** — Trener snage i kondicije (bivši UFC Performance Institute, American Top Team). Web stranica: [darustrong.com](https://www.darustrong.com). Članci i programi o sportski specifičnom S&C za MMA borce.

3. **Firas Zahabi** — Glavni trener, Tristar Gym. *JRE MMA Show #32* (Joe Rogan Experience), diskusija o filozofiji obima treninga naspram intenziteta. YouTube i podcast intervjui o periodizaciji za borce.

4. **Pavel Tsatsouline** — *Power to the People!*, 1999. *Simple & Sinister*, 2013 (revidirano 2019). StrongFirst metodologija — trening snage s malim brojem ponavljanja i visokom frekvencijom. Web stranica: [strongfirst.com](https://www.strongfirst.com).

5. **Alex Viada** — *The Hybrid Athlete*, 2015. Web stranica: [completehumanperformance.com](https://www.completehumanperformance.com). Metodologija konkurentnog treninga snage i izdržljivosti.

6. **Mike Boyle** — *New Functional Training for Sports*, 2. izdanje, Human Kinetics, 2016. Članci o funkcionalnom treningu i unilateralnom radu. Web stranica: [bodybyboyle.com](https://www.bodybyboyle.com).

7. **Cal Dietz** — *Triphasic Training: A Systematic Approach to Elite Speed and Explosive Strength Performance*, 2012. Web stranica: [triphasictraining.com](https://www.triphasictraining.com). Trening ekscentrične, izometrijske i koncentrične faze.

8. **Loren Landow** — *Ultimate Conditioning for Martial Arts*, Human Kinetics, 2016. Protokoli brzine, agilnosti i kondicije specifični za borilačke sportove.

9. **Eric Cressey** — *Simplified Shoulder Solutions* (video serija). Web stranica: [ericcressey.com](https://www.ericcressey.com). Zdravlje ramena, mobilnost i prevencija povreda za sportaše.

10. **Joe DeFranco** — *Westside for Skinny Bastards III (WS4SB III)*. Web stranica: [defrancostraining.com](https://www.defrancostraining.com). Gornji/donji split s metodama maksimalnog napora i repetitivnog napora prilagođen za sportaše.

---

## 2. Naučni radovi — Ishrana

1. **Reale R, Slater G, Cox GR, Dunican IC, Burke LM, et al.** — "ISSN position stand: nutrition for combat sports." *Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition*, 2025. DOI: [10.1080/15502783.2025.2467909](https://doi.org/10.1080/15502783.2025.2467909)

2. **Kasper AM, Cocking S, Cocber M, Sherwood M, et al.** — "Carbohydrate and fluid manipulation in combat sports." *Nutrients*, 2021. PMC: [PMC8334642](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8334642/)

---

## 3. Naučni radovi — Trening i periodizacija

1. **Wilson JM, Marin PJ, Rhea MR, Wilson SMC, Loenneke JP, Anderson JC.** — "Concurrent training: a meta-analysis examining interference of aerobic and resistance exercises." *Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research*, 2012; 26(8): 2293–2307. PMID: [22002517](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22002517/)

2. **Fyfe JJ, Bishop DJ, Stepto NK.** — "Interference between concurrent resistance and endurance exercise: molecular bases and the role of individual training variables." *Sports Medicine*, 2014; 44(6): 743–762. PMID: [24728927](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24728927/)

3. **Sabag A, Najafi A, Michael S, Esgin T, Halaki M, Hackett D.** — "The compatibility of concurrent high-intensity interval training and resistance training for muscular strength and hypertrophy: a systematic review and meta-analysis." *Journal of Sports Sciences*, 2018; 36(21): 2472–2483. PMID: [29809072](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29809072/)

4. **Franchini E, Cormack S, Takito MY, Hasan AAA, Yuri DA, et al.** — "Energy system contributions in combat sports: a systematic review." *International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health*, 2023. PMC: [PMC9961508](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9961508/)

5. **Tota Ł, Drwal T, Fischedick A, et al.** — "Conditioning training and its effects on preparation of MMA athletes." *International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health*, 2019; 16(9): 1495. PMC: [PMC6942465](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6942465/)

6. **Kostikiadis IN, Methenitis S, Tsoukos A, Veligekas P, Terzis G, Bogdanis GC.** — "The effect of short-term sport-specific strength and conditioning training on physical fitness of well-trained mixed martial arts athletes." *Journal of Sports Science and Medicine*, 2018; 17(3): 348–358. PMC: [PMC6090403](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6090403/)

7. **Schoenfeld BJ.** — "Periodization for MMA: bridging the gap between science and practice." Academia.edu. URL: [academia.edu](https://www.academia.edu) (pretraga: Schoenfeld periodization MMA)

8. **James LP, Haff GG, Kelly VG, Beckman EM.** — "Towards a determination of the physiological characteristics distinguishing successful mixed martial arts athletes: a systematic review of combat sport literature." *Sports Medicine*, ResearchGate. URL: [researchgate.net](https://www.researchgate.net) (pretraga: James strength conditioning MMA)

---

## 4. Naučni radovi — Oporavak

1. **Dupuy O, Douzi W, Theurot D, Bosquet L, Dugué B.** — "An evidence-based approach for choosing post-exercise recovery techniques to reduce markers of muscle damage, soreness, fatigue, and inflammation: a systematic review with meta-analysis." *Frontiers in Physiology*, 2020. PMID: [33092328](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33092328/)

2. **Souza DB, Delvaux F, Schwartz C, Croisier JL.** — "Recovery methods used by combat sport athletes: a scoping review." *Sports Medicine - Open*, 2025. PMID: [40378882](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40378882/)

3. **Carvalho L, Freitas TT, Pereira LA, Nakamura FY, Loturco I.** — "Fatigue markers and performance recovery in mixed martial arts athletes after competitive bouts." *Scientific Reports*, 2025. DOI: [10.1038/s41598-025-09719-z](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-09719-z)

---

## 5. Naučni radovi — Suplementacija

1. **Kreider RB, Kalman DS, Antonio J, Ziegenfuss TN, Wildman R, et al.** — "International Society of Sports Nutrition position stand: safety and efficacy of creatine supplementation in exercise, sport, and medicine." *Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition*, 2017; 14: 18. PMC: [PMC5469049](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5469049/)

2. **Guest NS, VanDusseldorp TA, Nelson MT, Grgic J, Schoenfeld BJ, et al.** — "International Society of Sports Nutrition position stand: caffeine and exercise performance." *Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition*, 2021; 18(1): 1. PMC: [PMC7777221](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7777221/)

3. **Maughan RJ, Burke LM, Dvorak J, Larson-Meyer DE, Peeling P, et al.** — "IOC consensus statement: dietary supplements and the high-performance athlete." *British Journal of Sports Medicine*, 2018; 52(7): 439–455. PMC: [PMC5867441](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5867441/)

4. **Ashtary-Larky D, Asbaghi O, Moosavian SP, et al.** — "Creatine supplementation in combat sports: a systematic review." *Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition*, 2025. PMID: [40755023](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40755023/)

5. **Li Z, Chen Y, Wang X, et al.** — "Effects of dietary supplements on physical performance in combat athletes: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials." *Scientific Reports*, 2024. DOI: [10.1038/s41598-024-84359-3](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-024-84359-3)

---

## 6. Naučni radovi — San i oporavak

1. **Vitale KC, Owens R, Hopkins SR, Malhotra A.** — "Sleep hygiene for optimizing recovery in athletes: review and recommendations." *International Journal of Sports Medicine*, 2019; 40(8): 535–543. PMC: [PMC6988893](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6988893/)

2. **Bonnar D, Lee S, Roane BM, et al.** — "Sleep and athletic performance: impacts on physical performance, mental health, injury risk, nutrition, and recovery." *Sleep Medicine Reviews*, 2023. PMC: [PMC9960533](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9960533/)

3. **Mah CD, Mah KE, Kezirian EJ, Dement WC.** — "The effects of sleep extension on the athletic performance of collegiate basketball players." *Sleep*, 2011; 34(7): 943–950. DOI: [10.5665/SLEEP.1132](https://doi.org/10.5665/SLEEP.1132)

---

## 7. Naučni radovi — Hidratacija

1. **Sawka MN, Burke LM, Eichner ER, Maughan RJ, Montain SJ, Stachenfeld NS.** — "American College of Sports Medicine position stand: exercise and fluid replacement." *Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise*, 2007; 39(2): 377–390. DOI: [10.1249/mss.0b013e31802ca597](https://doi.org/10.1249/mss.0b013e31802ca597)

---

## 8. Naučni radovi — Vitamini i minerali

1. **Chiang CM, Ismaeel A, Griffis RB, Weems S.** — "Effects of vitamin D supplementation on muscle strength in athletes: a systematic review." *Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research*, 2023. PMC: [PMC10768611](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC10768611/)

2. **Heffernan SM, Horner K, et al.** — "The role of vitamin D and magnesium in athletic performance and recovery." *Nutrients*, 2025; 17(10): 1655. DOI: [10.3390/nu17101655](https://doi.org/10.3390/nu17101655)

---

## 9. Organizacije

1. **UFC Performance Institute (UFCPI)** — Web stranica: [ufcpi.com](https://www.ufcpi.com). Faze treninga i grafikon periodizacije za MMA sportaše. *UFC Performance Institute's Training & Nutrition Guide*, 2018 (javno dostupan PDF). Istraživanja sportske nauke o profiliranju MMA sportaša, tjelesnom sastavu i referentnim vrijednostima performansi.
phase-1:
# Phase 1 — Base Camp (Weeks 1-3)

> **Phase:** Accumulation / Base Building | **Duration:** March 16 - April 5, 2026
> **Weight Target:** 89 kg → 85 kg (gradual deficit ~1.3 kg/week)
> **Training Focus:** Movement quality, baseline establishment, progressive overload
> **RPE Progression:** Week 1: 6-7 | Week 2: 7-8 | Week 3: 8-9
> **1RMs:** Squat 150 kg | Deadlift 180 kg | Bench 115 kg | OHP 75 kg

---

## Phase 1 Overview

Phase 1 builds the **aerobic and structural foundation** the entire camp depends on. The goal is not to destroy yourself in Week 1 — it's to establish patterns, dial in technique under moderate load, and begin a sustainable caloric deficit that will carry through all 9 weeks.

Strength work uses **linear progressive overload** across the 3 weeks. Loads start at 65-70% 1RM and climb to 80-85% by Week 3. MMA sessions focus on volume and technique accumulation — sparring is limited to once per week (Thursday) and starts at controlled intensity.

### Phase 1 Goals

- Establish 2x/day training rhythm (AM strength + PM MMA)
- Build aerobic base with 2-3 cardiac output sessions per week
- Progressive overload on all compound lifts
- One hard sparring session per week (Thursday)
- Begin caloric deficit (~500 kcal/day → ~0.5 kg/week from diet alone)
- Baseline conditioning test (Week 0 or early Week 1)
- Establish morning weight tracking habit

### Tempo Key

All strength movements use the following tempo notation unless otherwise noted:
- **3** = 3 sec eccentric (lowering)
- **1** = 1 sec pause at bottom
- **2** = 2 sec concentric (controlled lift)
- **0** = no pause at top

Week 3 shifts to **3-1-X-0** on compound lifts (X = explosive concentric) to prepare for Phase 2.

### Intensity Key

| Marker | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| 🟢 | Easy — recovery pace, low CNS demand |
| 🟡 | Moderate — working hard but controlled |
| 🔴 | Hard — high intensity, high CNS demand |

---

## Weekly Schedule Template

| Day | AM Session | PM Session |
|-----|-----------|------------|
| **Monday** | Strength Upper 🟡 | Striking 🔴 |
| **Tuesday** | Cardiac Output 🟢 | Grappling 🟡 |
| **Wednesday** | Strength Lower 🔴 | Light Technique 🟢 |
| **Thursday** | HIIT / Fight Circuit 🔴 | Sparring 🔴 |
| **Friday** | Strength Power 🟡 | Drilling 🟡 |
| **Saturday** | Conditioning 🟡 | Open Mat 🟢 |
| **Sunday** | REST | REST |

---

# Week 1 — Base Volume

> **Global RPE Target:** 6-7
> **Theme:** Establish training patterns, learn the rhythm, moderate intensity across the board.
> **Weight:** ~89 kg → ~88 kg

---

## Monday — Week 1

### AM — Strength: Upper Push/Pull (60 min) 🟡

**Warm-Up** (10 min) — Protocol 1: Upper Body Warm-Up (see Training Tools).

| Exercise | Sets x Reps | Load | Rest | Tempo | RPE | Notes |
|----------|-------------|------|------|-------|-----|-------|
| Bench Press | 4 x 6 | 75 kg (65%) | 2.5 min | 3-1-2-0 | 6 | Control the eccentric. Full ROM, scapulae retracted. |
| Barbell Row | 4 x 6 | 70 kg | 2.5 min | 3-1-2-0 | 6 | Pull to lower sternum. Torso stays still. No heaving. |
| Overhead Press | 3 x 8 | 45 kg (60%) | 2 min | 3-1-2-0 | 6 | Strict press. Brace core, press straight up. |
| Weighted Pull-Up | 3 x 6 | BW + 10 kg | 2 min | 3-1-2-0 | 6 | Dead hang start. Full extension at bottom. |
| Incline DB Press | 3 x 10 | 28 kg each | 90 s | 3-1-2-0 | 6 | 30-degree incline. Controlled path. |
| Face Pulls | 3 x 15 | Light cable | 60 s | 2-1-1-0 | 5 | Prehab. External rotation at top. |
| Pallof Press | 3 x 10/side | Band/cable | 60 s | 2-1-2-0 | 6 | Anti-rotation. Full arm extension. |

**Session Total:** 23 working sets, ~50 min

**Cool-Down** (5 min) — Pec doorway stretch 2x30s/side, banded lat stretch 2x30s/side, thoracic foam roll 60s.

---

### PM — MMA Striking (75 min) 🔴

**Warm-Up** (10 min) — Protocol 4: MMA Striking Warm-Up + Neck Isometrics.

| Block | Duration | Intensity | Description |
|-------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Shadow Boxing | 3 x 3 min (1 min rest) | Moderate | Establish stance, basic combos. Jab-cross, jab-cross-hook. Focus on hand return and footwork. |
| Pad Work — Fundamentals | 4 x 3 min (1 min rest) | Moderate-High | Coach feeds 3-4 strike combos. Focus on technique, snap, and returning to guard. |
| Heavy Bag — Combinations | 3 x 3 min (1 min rest) | Moderate | Work basic combinations at 70%. Focus on hip rotation and foot position. |
| Technical Sparring — Light | 2 x 3 min (1 min rest) | Light-Moderate | Striking only, 50-60% power. Focus on range management and defensive movement. |
| Body Shot Drill | 2 x 2 min (1 min rest) | Moderate | Partner drill: alternate attacking body with hooks and uppercuts, defend with elbows. |

**Cool-Down** (5 min) — Static stretching: shoulders, hip flexors. Light wrist circles. 1 min easy shadow boxing.

---

## Tuesday — Week 1

### AM — Cardiac Output (40 min) 🟢

**Warm-Up** (5 min) — Protocol 6: Conditioning Warm-Up, easy build-up.

| Block | Duration | Intensity | Notes |
|-------|----------|-----------|-------|
| Zone 2 Sustained | 40 min | 130-150 BPM | Assault bike, rower, or easy jog. Nasal breathing preferred. Conversational pace — if you can't talk, slow down. |

> **HR Target:** 130-150 BPM. This builds the aerobic engine that fuels recovery between rounds.

**Cool-Down** (5 min) — Walk 3 min, light stretching.

---

### PM — MMA Grappling (75 min) 🟡

**Warm-Up** (12 min) — Protocol 5: MMA Grappling Warm-Up + Neck Isometrics.

| Block | Duration | Intensity | Description |
|-------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Technique Instruction | 15 min | Low | Coach demonstrates technique of the day. Takedown entries or guard passes. |
| Positional Drilling — Guard | 3 x 5 min (1 min rest) | Moderate | Start in closed guard. Top works passes, bottom works sweeps/submissions. Reset on pass or sweep. |
| Positional Drilling — Takedowns | 3 x 3 min (1 min rest) | Moderate | Start standing. Single-leg and double-leg entries. 60-70% resistance. |
| Live Grappling | 3 x 5 min (1 min rest) | Moderate | Submission only (no strikes). Focus on position before submission. |

**Cool-Down** (5 min) — Partner stretching. Hip 90/90 holds 60s/side. Supine spinal twist 60s/side.

---

## Wednesday — Week 1

### AM — Strength: Lower + Posterior Chain (65 min) 🔴

**Warm-Up** (12 min) — Protocol 2: Lower Body Warm-Up.

| Exercise | Sets x Reps | Load | Rest | Tempo | RPE | Notes |
|----------|-------------|------|------|-------|-----|-------|
| Back Squat | 4 x 6 | 98 kg (65%) | 3 min | 3-1-2-0 | 6 | Full depth. Brace hard, spread the floor. |
| Romanian Deadlift | 4 x 8 | 100 kg | 2.5 min | 3-1-2-0 | 6 | Deep hinge. Feel hamstring stretch. Bar stays on legs. |
| RFESS (Bulgarian Split Squat) | 3 x 8/leg | 20 kg DBs | 90 s/leg | 3-1-2-0 | 6-7 | Rear foot on bench. Drive through front heel. |
| Glute-Ham Raise or Nordic Curl | 3 x 6 | BW (assisted) | 2 min | 3-0-2-0 | 6 | Eccentric focus. Control the descent. |
| Copenhagen Plank | 3 x 20s/side | BW | 60 s | Hold | 6 | Top foot on bench. Straight line from head to feet. |
| Dead Bug | 3 x 10/side | BW | 60 s | Slow | 5 | Low back glued to floor. Exhale on extension. |
| Farmer's Carry | 3 x 40m | 36 kg/hand | 90 s | Walk | 6 | Stay tall. Don't lean. Squeeze handles hard. |

**Session Total:** 23 working sets, ~55 min

**Cool-Down** (5 min) — Hip flexor stretch 2x30s/side, hamstring stretch 2x30s/side, foam roll quads and glutes 2 min.

---

### PM — Light Technique / Open Mat (60 min) 🟢

**Warm-Up** (10 min) — Light movement, shadow boxing, basic grappling movements.

| Block | Duration | Intensity | Description |
|-------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Technique Review | 20 min | Low | Review and drill techniques from the week. No resistance. Focus on smooth, correct movement. |
| Flow Rolling | 3 x 5 min (1 min rest) | Easy | Rolling at 40-50%. No submissions with force. Movement practice only. |
| Shadow Boxing + Visualization | 2 x 3 min | Easy | Light movement. Visualize an opponent. Practice entries and exits. |

**Cool-Down** (5 min) — Mobility flow: Cat-cow, pigeon pose, thoracic rotations.

---

## Thursday — Week 1

### AM — HIIT / Fight Circuit (35 min) 🔴

**Warm-Up** (8 min) — Protocol 6: Conditioning Warm-Up.

**Circuit: 4 rounds, 5 exercises, 30s work / 20s rest, 2 min between rounds**

| Station | Exercise | Target |
|---------|----------|--------|
| 1 | Assault Bike Sprint | Max calories — all-out |
| 2 | Med Ball Overhead Slam | Power — maximum force each rep |
| 3 | Sprawl-to-Burpee | Speed — max clean reps |
| 4 | Battle Rope Slams (alternating) | Endurance — maintain rhythm |
| 5 | KB Swings (24 kg) | Hip snap — quality over speed |

**Session Total:** 4 rounds × 4:10 = ~17 min work + rest between rounds = ~25 min

**Cool-Down** (5 min) — Walk 3 min, deep breathing, light stretching.

---

### PM — MMA Sparring (90 min) 🔴

**Warm-Up** (15 min) — Protocol 4: Striking Warm-Up + Protocol 5 elements (hip escapes, pummeling) + Neck Isometrics.

| Block | Duration | Intensity | Description |
|-------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| Pad Work — Sharp Combos | 3 x 3 min (1 min rest) | Moderate-High | Get sharp before sparring. Short combos, exit angles. |
| Light Technical Sparring | 2 x 3 min (1 min rest) | 50-60% | Striking only. Focus on distance and timing. No hard shots. |
| MMA Sparring | 3 x 5 min (2 min rest) | 70-75% | Full MMA rules. Controlled intensity — learning week. Focus on implementing technique. |
| Positional Sparring — Cage | 2 x 3 min (1 min rest) | 60-70% | Start in clinch against cage. Work pummeling, trips, wall work. |

**Post-Sparring:** Neuro check (see Fight Prep). Ice any impacted areas.

**Cool-Down** (10 min) — Extended cool-down. Partner stretching. Foam roll. Ice as needed.

---

## Friday — Week 1

### AM — Strength: Full Body Power (55 min) 🟡

**Warm-Up** (11 min) — Protocol 3: Power Day Warm-Up.

| Exercise | Sets x Reps | Load | Rest | Tempo | RPE | Notes |
|----------|-------------|------|------|-------|-----|-------|
| Hang Clean | 5 x 3 | 65 kg | 2.5 min | Explosive | 6-7 | Triple extension. Fast elbows. Catch in quarter squat. |
| Box Jump | 4 x 3 | 24" box | 2 min | Explosive | 6 | Full hip extension on takeoff. Soft landing. Step down. |
| Push Press | 4 x 5 | 50 kg (67%) | 2 min | 2-0-X-0 | 6-7 | Quick dip, aggressive drive. Legs do the work. |
| Broad Jump | 3 x 3 | BW | 90 s | Explosive | 6 | Max distance. Stick the landing. |
| Med Ball Rotational Slam | 3 x 6/side | 14 lb | 90 s | Explosive | 6 | Hip-first rotation. Slam with intent. |
| Single-Leg RDL | 3 x 8/leg | 20 kg DB | 90 s | 3-1-2-0 | 6 | Balance and posterior chain. Control the hinge. |
| Ab Wheel Rollout | 3 x 8 | BW | 60 s | 3-0-2-0 | 6 | From knees. Full extension without lumbar sag. |
| Neck Harness | 2 x 15 | 2.5 kg | 60 s | 2-1-2-0 | 5 | Week 1 intro. Flexion and extension only. Slow and controlled. |

**Session Total:** 27 working sets, ~45 min

**Cool-Down** (5 min) — Hip flexor stretch, shoulder band dislocates, thoracic foam roll.

---

### PM — MMA Drilling (75 min) 🟡

**Warm-Up** (10 min) — Protocol 4: Striking + movement drills.

| Block | Duration | Intensity | Description |
|-------|----------|-----------|-------------|

| 3 | 85 kg | ~2,600 kcal | 190g (2.2g/kg) | 260g | 85g |

> **Deficit Strategy:** ~500 kcal/day below TDEE. Prioritize protein to preserve muscle. Carbs concentrated around training sessions (pre/post AM and PM). Fats from whole food sources.

---

## Phase 1 Benchmarks

By end of Week 3, you should be able to:

- [ ] Complete all sessions 2x/day for 6 days without excessive fatigue
- [ ] Squat 80% 1RM for 4 reps with good form and explosive concentric
- [ ] Deadlift 80% 1RM for 4 reps
- [ ] Complete 5 rounds of HIIT circuit without form breakdown
- [ ] Spar 4 × 5-min MMA rounds at 80% and recover between rounds
- [ ] Maintain Zone 2 HR for 45 minutes without drift above 150 BPM
- [ ] Morning weight trending toward 85 kg
- [ ] RHR stable or decreasing from Week 1 baseline

**If all benchmarks are met:** Proceed to Phase 2 as planned.
**If 2+ benchmarks are missed:** Extend Phase 1 by 1 week (reduce Phase 3 by 1 week to maintain fight date).
phase-2:
# Phase 2 — Intensification (Weeks 4-6)

> **Phase:** Transmutation / Peak Volume | **Duration:** April 6 - April 26, 2026
> **Weight Target:** 85 kg → 81 kg (continue ~1.3 kg/week deficit)
> **Training Focus:** Peak training volume, hardest conditioning, increased sparring
> **RPE Progression:** Week 4: 7-8 | Week 5: 8-9 | Week 6: 8-9 (peak)
> **1RMs:** Squat 150 kg | Deadlift 180 kg | Bench 115 kg | OHP 75 kg

---

## Phase 2 Overview

This is the **hardest phase of camp**. Everything escalates: sparring frequency doubles to twice per week, conditioning sessions layer on top of each other, and fight simulation circuits appear regularly. Strength work shifts from progression to **maintenance** — loads stay at 80-85% 1RM with reduced volume so that recovery resources can be directed toward MMA-specific work and sparring.

### Key Changes from Phase 1

| Element | Phase 1 | Phase 2 |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Sparring | 1x/week (Thu) | **2x/week (Tue PM + Thu PM)** |
| Strength Goal | Progressive overload | **Maintenance (80-85% 1RM)** |
| Conditioning | Single session days | **Double conditioning on some days** |
| Tempo | 3-1-1-0 (varied) | **3-1-X-0 (explosive concentric throughout)** |
| Rest Periods | Standard (2-3 min) | **Tightened on conditioning work** |
| Accessories | General | **MMA-specific replacements** |
| Fight Simulation | Occasional | **Frequent (2-3x/week)** |
| Opponent Drilling | None | **Integrated into technique sessions** |

### Tempo Key

All strength movements use **3-1-X-0** unless otherwise noted:
- **3** = 3 sec eccentric (lowering)
- **1** = 1 sec pause at bottom
- **X** = eXplosive concentric (as fast as possible)
- **0** = no pause at top

### Intensity Key

| Marker | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| 🟢 | Easy — recovery pace, low CNS demand |
| 🟡 | Moderate — working hard but controlled |
| 🔴 | Hard — high intensity, high CNS demand, full effort |

---

## Weekly Schedule Template

|-----|-----------|------------|
| **Monday** | Strength Upper 🟡 | Striking 🔴 |
| **Tuesday** | Cardiac Output 🟢 | Sparring 🔴 |
| **Wednesday** | Strength Lower 🟡 | Light Technique 🟢 |
| **Thursday** | HIIT 🔴 | Full MMA Sparring 🔴 |
| **Friday** | Strength Power 🟡 | Drilling 🟡 |
| **Saturday** | Fight Conditioning (peak) 🔴 | Open Mat 🟡 |
| **Sunday** | REST | REST |

## Mid-Phase Checkpoint — After Week 5

Complete the following assessments on Sunday evening or Monday morning before Week 6 begins.

### Body Composition Check

| Metric | Target | Action if Off-Track |
|--------|--------|---------------------|
| Weight | ~81.5 kg | If above 82.5 kg: tighten nutrition, add 10 min Zone 2 to Tuesday/Saturday AM. If below 81 kg: increase carbs slightly — do not undercut performance. |
| Visual | Noticeably leaner than Phase 1 start | If holding water: check sodium, sleep quality, and stress. |

### Performance Markers

| Test | Minimum Standard | Notes |
|------|-----------------|-------|
| Resting HR | Stable or decreasing from Phase 1 | If elevated >5 BPM: overreaching — reduce Week 6 volume 10%. |
| HRV | Stable or within normal range | Significant drop = overreaching signal. |
| Grip Strength | No decline from Phase 1 baseline | Drop = CNS fatigue indicator. |
| Mood / Motivation | Manageable | Some fatigue is normal. If motivation is critically low, reduce Week 6 sparring to 1x. |
| Sleep Quality | 7+ hours average | If poor: prioritize sleep hygiene over extra training. |
| Injury Check | No new injuries | Any joint pain, persistent soreness, or swelling = modify. Do not push through injuries this close to fight. |

### Decision Point

| Condition | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| All markers green | Proceed with full Week 6 programming |
| Resting HR elevated or HRV suppressed | Reduce Week 6 conditioning volume by 15-20%. Maintain sparring. |
| Joint pain or injury | Modify around the issue. If serious, convert Week 6 to modified taper. Consult medical. |
| Weight ahead of schedule (<81 kg) | Add carbs to performance meals. Prioritize recovery. |
| Weight behind schedule (>83 kg) | Add 10 min Zone 2 daily. Tighten nutrition. DO NOT crash diet. |
| Mental burnout | Reduce volume 10%. Add fun training — open mat, flow rolling, playful sparring. |

## Key Adaptations Expected by End of Phase 2

| Quality | Expected Outcome |
|---------|-----------------|
| Aerobic Base | Maintained. Cardiac output work continues throughout. |
| Anaerobic Capacity | Significantly improved. Fight simulation circuits and HIIT at peak levels. |
| Strength | Maintained at current 1RMs. No regression despite caloric deficit. |
| Power | Maintained or slightly improved through explosive tempo work. |
| Fight Readiness | High. Two sparring sessions per week with full MMA rounds and adversity training. |
| Mental Toughness | Forged. Shark tanks, fatigue rounds, and championship overtime build deep reserves. |
| Weight | ~81 kg. On track for 77 kg fight weight with 4 weeks remaining for water cut strategy. |
| Gameplan | Polished. Opponent-specific drilling integrated throughout all sessions. |

## What Changes in Phase 3 (Taper)

After this peak phase, Phase 3 will:
- **Reduce volume** by 30-40% while maintaining intensity
- **Cut sparring** back to once per week, then to technical only
- **Eliminate fight simulation circuits** — they have served their purpose
- **Maintain strength** with reduced sets (2-3 sets instead of 4)
- **Focus on sharpness** — technique drilling replaces volume work
- **Begin water cut strategy** in the final 7-10 days
- **Prioritize recovery** — sleep, nutrition, and mental preparation take center stage

> **The hardest work is done.** Everything from here is about arriving at fight night sharp, fresh, and dangerous.
phase-3:
# Phase 3 — Sharpening (Weeks 7-8)

> **Phase:** Realization / Taper | **Duration:** April 27 - May 10, 2026
> **Weight Target:** 81 kg → 79 kg (within water-cut range of 77 kg)
> **Training Focus:** Technical sharpness, speed work, game plan drilling, volume reduction
> **RPE:** 7-8 (controlled intensity, reduced volume)
> **Key Changes:** Strength 2x/week (down from 3), no hard sparring in Week 8, speed/timing replaces volume

---

## Phase 3 Overview

This phase is about **subtraction, not addition**. You are not building fitness here — you are sharpening what you already have and allowing your body to supercompensate. Every session has a purpose. If it doesn't make you sharper for fight night, cut it.

### Key Principles

- **Strength drops to 2 sessions/week** (Monday and Wednesday only — Friday power session is removed)
- **Loads drop to 70-75% of 1RM** with emphasis on bar speed and explosiveness
- **No hard sparring in Week 8** — light technical sparring only
- **Week 7** — moderate sparring is still permitted (controlled rounds, trusted partners only)
- **Conditioning becomes shorter and sharper** — intervals under 10 seconds, full recovery between sets
- **Game plan specific drilling increases** — positional sparring, situational reps, entries and exits
- **Mental preparation begins** — daily visualization, fight strategy review, routine-setting
- **Water loading protocol discussion** — coordinate with coach and reference weight-management section

#### PM: Light MMA — Game Plan Review (50 min)

**Begin discussing water loading protocol with coach.** Reference Phase 4 for the full protocol starting Sunday, May 11.

## Phase 3 Summary

| Parameter | Week 7 | Week 8 |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Strength Sessions | 2 (Mon, Wed) | 2 (Mon, Wed) — final sessions |
| Strength Volume | 4x3 main lifts + 2-3 accessories | 3x2 main lifts + minimal accessories |
| Strength Load | 70-75% 1RM | 70-72.5% 1RM |
| Sparring | Moderate (Friday, 5 rounds) | Light technical only (Monday) |
| Conditioning | Short intervals (2 sessions) | Minimal intervals (1 session) |
| MMA Skills | Game plan focus, positional sparring | Technical rehearsal, shadow boxing |
| Mental Prep | Visualization begins, strategy review | Daily visualization, logistics finalized |
| Estimated Body Weight | ~80-81 kg | ~79-80 kg |
| Recovery Focus | High | Very high |

---

## Notes for Coach

1. **Monitor fatigue closely.** If the athlete reports unusual soreness, sleep disruption, or mood changes, cut volume further.
2. **Sparring partners in Week 7** should be briefed — controlled rounds, no ego, game plan focus.
3. **Week 8 has zero hard sparring.** This is non-negotiable. The fight is won in the weeks before, not the week of.
4. **Weight should be trending toward 79-80 kg** naturally by end of Week 8. If still above 81 kg, consult with nutritionist before beginning water cut.
5. **Transition to Phase 4 (Fight Week)** begins Sunday, May 11.

# Phase 4 — Fight Week (Week 9)

> **Phase:** Fight Week | **Duration:** May 11 - May 16, 2026
> **Weight:** 79 kg → 77 kg (water cut)
> **Focus:** Light technical work, weight management, mental preparation
> **Training:** Minimal — shadow boxing, visualization, light movement only

---

## Fight Week Overview

This is not a training week. This is a **preparation and execution** week. The work is done. Your only jobs now are:

1. Make weight safely
2. Stay mentally sharp
3. Arrive at the fight healthy, hydrated, and confident

The 2 kg water cut from 79 kg to 77 kg is a **mild cut** and should be manageable with proper water loading and sodium manipulation. Follow the protocols below exactly as planned with your coach.

---

## Water Loading Protocol

Begin water loading on Sunday. The goal is to upregulate your body's water excretion mechanisms by drinking large volumes, then sharply reducing intake so the body continues flushing water even when intake drops.

| Day | Date | Water Intake | Notes |
|-----|------|-------------|-------|
| Sunday | May 11 | 8 L | Begin water loading. Spread across waking hours. |
| Monday | May 12 | 8 L | Continue loading. Urine should be clear and frequent. |
| Tuesday | May 13 | 6 L | Begin tapering water. Body still excreting at high rate. |
| Wednesday | May 14 | 4 L | Further reduction. Noticeable water loss begins. |
| Thursday | May 15 | 2 L | Minimal water. Sip throughout day. Stop by evening. |
| Friday | May 16 | Sips only | Small sips until weigh-in. Full rehydration after. |

**Key Rules:**
- Spread water intake evenly across the day (do not chug large volumes at once)
- Distilled water is preferred during loading days (lower mineral content)
- Track all fluid intake — use a measured bottle
- Weigh yourself morning and evening to track progress
- If weight is not trending on schedule, consult coach before adjusting

---

## Sodium Manipulation Protocol

Sodium manipulation works alongside water loading. High sodium early causes water retention; dropping sodium causes the body to flush stored water.

| Day | Date | Sodium Intake | Food Notes |
|-----|------|--------------|------------|
| Sunday | May 11 | Normal (~3,000-4,000 mg) | Eat normally, add salt to food |
| Monday | May 12 | Normal (~3,000-4,000 mg) | Continue normal salting |
| Tuesday | May 13 | Reduced (~1,500-2,000 mg) | Begin reducing — avoid processed food, no added salt |
| Wednesday | May 14 | Low (~500-1,000 mg) | Plain foods only — rice, chicken, vegetables, no seasoning |
| Thursday | May 15 | Minimal (~200-500 mg) | Very plain food, small portions, no added salt |
| Friday | May 16 | None until post-weigh-in | No food until after weigh-in |

**Carbohydrate Notes:**
- Sunday-Tuesday: Normal carb intake
- Wednesday: Reduce carbs to ~100g (reduces glycogen-bound water)
- Thursday: Reduce carbs to ~50g
- Friday: No food until post-weigh-in

---

| 10:00 AM | Water: 1 L | Sip steadily |

| 2:00 PM | Water: 1 L | |

| 4:00 PM | Water: final sips | Stop water intake by evening |

| 8:00 PM | No more food or water | Done for the night |

**If over 77.5 kg at evening weigh-in:**
- A warm bath (20 min) can shed 0.5-1 kg
- Do NOT use sauna unless supervised by experienced coach
- Light sweat walk in warm clothing (15-20 min maximum)

**If at or below 77 kg at evening weigh-in:**
- Have a few small sips of water
- Relax — you are on weight

---

### Friday, May 16 — FIGHT DAY

| Time | Activity | Details |
|------|----------|---------|
| 6:00 AM | Wake up, weigh in (private) | Final private weight check — confirm on or under 77 kg |
| 6:00-8:00 AM | Sips of water only | Very small sips if needed, do not drink freely |
| 8:00 AM (est.) | **OFFICIAL WEIGH-IN** | Step on scale, make weight, done |
| **Immediately after weigh-in** | **BEGIN REHYDRATION** | See rehydration protocol below |
| 8:15 AM | Electrolyte drink (500 mL) | Oral rehydration solution — sip, do not chug |
| 8:30 AM | Small snack | Banana + white bread with honey |
| 9:00 AM | Electrolyte drink (500 mL) | Continue sipping |
| 9:30 AM | Pre-fight Meal 1 | See meal plan below |
| 11:00 AM | Electrolyte drink (500 mL) | |
| 11:30 AM | Pre-fight Meal 2 | See meal plan below |
| 1:00 PM | Sipping water/electrolytes | Continue rehydrating steadily |
| 2:00 PM | Light snack | Rice cake with honey, banana |
| 3:00-4:00 PM | Rest / nap if possible | Stay off feet, relax, conserve energy |
| 4:00 PM | Final pre-fight snack | Small, easily digestible — see meal plan |
| 5:00 PM | Begin fight prep | Arrive at venue, settle in, start mental routine |
| 5:30 PM | Light warm-up | Shadow boxing (5 min), dynamic stretches, pad work (2-3 min with corner) |
| 6:00 PM (est.) | **FIGHT TIME** | Execute the game plan. Trust your preparation. |

> **Note:** Adjust times based on actual weigh-in and fight schedule. The structure remains the same regardless of specific times.

---

## Rehydration Protocol (Post-Weigh-In)

The goal is to rehydrate from ~77 kg back toward ~79-80 kg before the fight. You have approximately 8-10 hours to rehydrate.

| Timeframe | Fluid | Food | Target |
|-----------|-------|------|--------|
| 0-30 min post-weigh-in | 500 mL ORS (oral rehydration solution) | Banana, white bread + honey | Begin absorption |
| 30-90 min | 500 mL ORS, sip steadily | Pre-fight Meal 1 (see below) | |
| 90 min - 3 hours | 500 mL water/electrolyte | Pre-fight Meal 2 (see below) | ~1.5 L consumed |
| 3-6 hours | Sip water/electrolytes as desired | Light snacks as desired | ~2-2.5 L consumed |
| 6-8 hours | Small sips only | Final small snack (2 hrs before fight) | ~3 L total consumed |

**Rehydration Rules:**
- **NEVER chug water.** Sip steadily. Chugging causes bloating, nausea, and poor absorption.
- Use an oral rehydration solution (ORS) or add electrolyte tabs to water (Pedialyte, LMNT, or similar)
- Do NOT drink plain water only — you need sodium and potassium to retain fluid
- Stop drinking large volumes 2 hours before fight time
- Target total fluid intake: 2.5-3.5 L between weigh-in and fight
- Monitor urine color — aim for pale yellow, not clear (clear means you're flushing, not retaining)

---

## Pre-Fight Meal Plan

All food should be **easily digestible, familiar, and not gas-producing.** Do not eat anything new on fight day.

### Pre-Fight Meal 1 (~90 min post-weigh-in)

| Food | Amount | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| White rice | 1.5 cups cooked | Easy carbs, familiar |
| Grilled chicken breast | 120g | Lean protein |
| Banana | 1 medium | Potassium source |
| Honey | 1 tbsp (on rice or bread) | Quick energy |

### Pre-Fight Meal 2 (~3 hours post-weigh-in)

| Food | Amount | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| White rice or white pasta | 1 cup cooked | Carb loading |
| Lean protein (chicken/fish) | 100g | |
| White bread | 1-2 slices | Easily digestible carbs |
| Small amount of fruit | Banana or melon | |

### Final Pre-Fight Snack (~2 hours before fight)

| Food | Amount | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| Rice cake | 2 | Light, easy carbs |
| Honey | 1 tbsp | Quick energy |
| Banana | Half | |
| Sips of electrolyte drink | 100-200 mL | |

**Foods to AVOID on fight day:**
- Dairy (bloating, gas)
- High fiber foods (beans, whole grains, raw vegetables)
- Spicy food
- Fatty or fried food
- Anything new or unusual
- Large volumes of any single food
- Caffeine in excess (one small coffee is fine if habitual)

---

## Mental Preparation Checklist

Complete these tasks across the final week:

### Strategy (Complete by Wednesday, May 14)
- [ ] Review game plan with coach — know your A-plan and B-plan
- [ ] Identify opponent's 3 biggest threats and your counter for each
- [ ] Identify your 3 best weapons and the setups to land them
- [ ] Know your Round 1 opening — first 30 seconds are scripted
- [ ] Know your clinch strategy (initiate vs. disengage)
- [ ] Know your ground strategy (top game vs. get-up plan)

### Visualization (Daily from Thursday, May 8 onward)
- [ ] Visualize the walk-out — music, crowd, corner team
- [ ] Visualize touching gloves, opening stance, first exchange
- [ ] Visualize landing your best combinations
- [ ] Visualize recovering from adversity (getting hit, taken down)
- [ ] Visualize executing your game plan under pressure
- [ ] Visualize winning — hand raised, celebration

### Emotional Readiness
- [ ] Accept nervousness as normal and useful (adrenaline is fuel)
- [ ] Identify your pre-fight routine (music, warm-up, breathing)
- [ ] Practice box breathing (4-4-4-4) — use it between rounds
- [ ] Have a "reset word" for when things get chaotic in the fight
- [ ] Talk to your corner about communication — keep it simple (1-2 cues max between rounds)

---

## Fight Day Bag Checklist

Pack this bag by **Thursday evening, May 15.**

### Competition Gear
- [ ] MMA gloves (competition approved)
- [ ] Mouthguard (primary + backup)
- [ ] Groin protector / cup
- [ ] MMA shorts (competition approved, no pockets, no zippers)
- [ ] Rash guard (if required by promotion)
- [ ] Hand wraps (2 pairs)
- [ ] Athletic tape

### Warm-Up / Corner
- [ ] Training gloves or pads (for corner warm-up)
- [ ] Vaseline (for cornerman to apply)
- [ ] Ice bags (ask venue or bring cooler)
- [ ] Towels (2-3)
- [ ] Gauze and tape (corner supplies)
- [ ] Adrenaline / epinephrine swabs (if allowed — ask corner)
- [ ] Enswell (cold compress for swelling)

### Nutrition / Hydration
- [ ] Electrolyte solution/powder (Pedialyte, LMNT, or similar)
- [ ] Water bottles (at least 2, filled)
- [ ] Bananas (2-3)
- [ ] White rice (pre-cooked in container)
- [ ] Honey packets
- [ ] Rice cakes
- [ ] White bread
- [ ] Pre-cooked chicken breast (in container, keep cool)

### Personal
- [ ] Photo ID (required for weigh-in)
- [ ] Medical clearance / fight license documentation
- [ ] Insurance documentation (if required)
- [ ] Phone + charger
- [ ] Headphones + fight playlist loaded
- [ ] Change of clothes (post-fight)
- [ ] Flip-flops / slides
- [ ] Toiletries (deodorant, etc.)
- [ ] Cash (for incidentals)

### Recovery (Post-Fight)
- [ ] Extra food for after the fight (whatever you want — you earned it)
- [ ] More electrolytes
- [ ] Compression gear (optional)
- [ ] Arnica gel or anti-inflammatory cream

---

## Warning Signs During the Water Cut

Monitor for these warning signs throughout the week. **If any STOP signs appear, halt the cut immediately and seek medical advice.**

### Normal / Expected
| Symptom | Notes |
|---------|-------|
| Mild thirst | Expected from Wednesday onward |
| Slightly reduced energy | Normal, conserve energy |
| Darker urine | Expected as water intake drops |
| Mild irritability | Normal — manage with breathing exercises |
| Slight headache (brief) | Can occur, usually resolves |
| Dry mouth | Expected Thursday/Friday |

### Caution — Slow Down
| Symptom | Action |
|---------|--------|
| Persistent headache | Small sips of electrolyte solution, reassess in 30 min |
| Mild dizziness when standing | Sit down, sip electrolytes, move slowly |
| Muscle cramps | Electrolytes needed — small sips of ORS |
| Extreme irritability / mood swings | Rest, breathe, reduce any remaining activity |
| Poor sleep quality | Accept it, stay in bed, rest even if not sleeping |

### STOP — Seek Help Immediately
| Symptom | Action |
|---------|--------|
| Severe dizziness or fainting | **STOP CUT.** Drink electrolytes. Call coach/doctor. |
| Rapid or irregular heartbeat | **STOP CUT.** Seek medical attention immediately. |
| Confusion or disorientation | **STOP CUT.** This is dangerous. Rehydrate now. |
| Inability to stand | **STOP CUT.** Medical emergency possible. |
| Chest pain | **STOP CUT. CALL EMERGENCY SERVICES.** |
| Vomiting (unable to keep fluids down) | **STOP CUT.** Seek medical help — dehydration will worsen. |
| Seizures or muscle spasms (severe) | **STOP CUT. CALL EMERGENCY SERVICES.** |

> **Remember:** No fight is worth your health. A 2 kg water cut is mild and should be straightforward. If it becomes anything more than mildly uncomfortable, something is wrong. Speak up.

---

## Phase 4 Summary

| Day | Date | Training | Water | Sodium | Key Focus |
|-----|------|----------|-------|--------|-----------|
| Sunday | May 11 | Last light session (40 min) | 8 L | Normal | Water loading begins |
| Monday | May 12 | Shadow boxing (20 min) | 8 L | Normal | Continue loading |
| Tuesday | May 13 | Light walk + movement (30 min) | 6 L | Reduced | Begin sodium reduction |
| Wednesday | May 14 | No training | 4 L | Low | Mental prep, reduce carbs |
| Thursday | May 15 | No training | 2 L | Minimal | Final weight check, pack bag |
| Friday | May 16 | **FIGHT DAY** | Sips → rehydrate | None → normal | Make weight, rehydrate, fight |

---

## Final Note

The work is done. Eight weeks of preparation have built your body, sharpened your skills, and hardened your mind. Fight week is about executing the plan, making weight safely, and showing up ready.

Trust your preparation. Trust your corner. Trust yourself.

Go get it.
weight-management:
# Weight Management

**Fight Date:** May 16, 2026 | **Weight Class:** 77 kg | **Camp Duration:** 9 weeks

---

## 1. Weight Cut Overview

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Starting weight | 89 kg |
| Target weigh-in weight | 77 kg |
| Total cut | 12 kg |
| Chronic dieting (Weeks 1-8) | ~9 kg (fat loss + glycogen depletion) |
| Acute water cut (Week 9) | ~3 kg |
| Camp duration | 9 weeks |

**Strategy:** Lose the majority of weight through sustained caloric deficit over 8 weeks, preserving as much muscle and performance as possible. Reserve the final 3 kg for a water/sodium manipulation protocol in fight week. This keeps the acute water cut within safe limits (~3.8% of body weight at 79 kg).

> **WARNING:** A 12 kg total cut is aggressive. This requires strict dietary compliance, consistent monitoring, and ideally supervision from a sports nutritionist experienced with combat athletes. If weight loss stalls or performance degrades significantly, reassess the target weight class.

---

## 2. Per-Phase Caloric Targets

| Phase | Weeks | Caloric Deficit | Training Day kcal | Rest Day kcal | Target Weight (end of phase) | Notes |
|-------|-------|----------------|-------------------|---------------|------------------------------|-------|
| **Phase 1 — Base Cut** | 1-3 | ~500 kcal/day | ~2,600 | ~2,100 | 85 kg | Establish deficit, maintain training intensity. ~1.3 kg/week loss. |
| **Phase 2 — Accelerated Cut** | 4-6 | ~600 kcal/day | ~2,400 | ~1,900 | 81 kg | Slightly deeper deficit. Increase protein to offset muscle loss. ~1.3 kg/week loss. |
| **Phase 3 — Final Trim** | 7-8 | ~500 kcal/day | ~2,400 | ~2,000 | 79 kg | Reduce deficit slightly to protect performance before fight. Begin water loading prep at end of Week 8. ~1 kg/week loss. |
| **Phase 4 — Fight Week** | 9 | Water/sodium manipulation | Minimal structured meals | — | 77 kg | Glycogen depletion + water cut for final ~3 kg. Food intake is minimal and strategic. |

**Key principles:**
- Protein stays at 2.3 g/kg throughout (higher than maintenance to preserve lean mass during deficit)
- Deficit is created primarily by reducing carbohydrates and fats — never by cutting protein
- Training day calories are always higher than rest day calories (carb cycling)

---

## 3. Weight Cut Timeline

Target weight at the end of each week (weighed fasted, morning, same scale):

| Week | Target Weight (kg) | Cumulative Loss (kg) | Phase | Notes |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| Start | 89.0 | 0.0 | — | Camp begins |
| 1 | 87.7 | 1.3 | Phase 1 | Initial water/glycogen drop may be faster |
| 2 | 86.4 | 2.6 | Phase 1 | Steady loss |
| 3 | 85.0 | 4.0 | Phase 1 | End of Phase 1 — reassess |
| 4 | 83.7 | 5.3 | Phase 2 | Deeper deficit begins |
| 5 | 82.3 | 6.7 | Phase 2 | Mid-camp check — conditioning test |
| 6 | 81.0 | 8.0 | Phase 2 | End of Phase 2 — reassess |
| 7 | 80.0 | 9.0 | Phase 3 | Protect performance, moderate deficit |
| 8 | 79.0 | 10.0 | Phase 3 | Water loading prep begins end of week |
| 9 | 77.0 | 12.0 | Phase 4 (Fight Week) | Water/sodium cut for final 2-3 kg |

> **If you are more than 1 kg behind target at any checkpoint**, do NOT increase the deficit beyond 600 kcal/day. Instead, add 20-30 minutes of low-intensity steady-state cardio (walking, cycling) on 2-3 rest days. Crash dieting destroys training quality and fight performance.

---

## 4. Macronutrient Framework

### Per-Kilogram Targets

| Nutrient | g/kg | Notes |
|----------|------|-------|
| **Protein** | 2.3 | Higher than maintenance (was 2.1) to preserve muscle during deficit. ~185 g constant. |
| **Carbs** | Varies by day type | Cycled by training demand — see table below |
| **Fat** | 0.8-1.0 | Minimum for hormonal health. ~65-80 g depending on phase. |

### Daily Totals by Day Type (Phase 1-2 Example at ~83 kg)

| Day Type | Protein | Carbs | Fat | kcal | Carb g/kg |
|----------|---------|-------|-----|------|-----------|
| **Training Day** | 185 g | 280-300 g | 70 g | ~2,400-2,600 | 3.4-3.6 |
| **Double Session Day** | 185 g | 320 g | 75 g | ~2,700 | 3.9 |
| **Rest Day** | 185 g | 170-200 g | 65 g | ~1,900-2,100 | 2.0-2.4 |

**Adjustments as weight drops:**
- Protein stays at 185 g constant (this becomes a higher g/kg as weight decreases — protective)
- Carbs are the primary lever — reduce by 20-30 g per phase as needed
- Fat should not drop below 0.8 g/kg (~65 g at 80 kg) to maintain testosterone and recovery

---

## 5. Meal Timing

### Pre-Training (2-3 hours before)

| Nutrient | Target | Grams | Goal |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Carbs | 1-1.5 g/kg | 80-120 g | Top off glycogen without excess calories |
| Protein | 0.3-0.4 g/kg | 25-35 g | Start muscle protein synthesis |
| Fat | Low | < 10 g | Speed gastric emptying |

### Pre-Training Snack (30-60 min before)

- **30-40 g fast-digesting carbs** — banana, white rice cake with honey, sports drink
- Keep fat and fiber minimal to avoid GI distress during training

### Intra-Workout (double sessions or any session > 90 min)

- **30-60 g carbs per hour** — sports drink, gummy bears, highly branched cyclic dextrin
- **Electrolytes** — 500-700 mg sodium per liter of fluid
- Sip continuously; do not chug between rounds
- During a cut, intra-workout carbs are even more critical to protect training quality

### Post-Training (within 1-2 hours)

| Nutrient | Target | Grams | Goal |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Protein | 0.3-0.5 g/kg | 25-40 g | Kickstart recovery/MPS |
| Carbs | 1-1.2 g/kg | 80-100 g | Replenish glycogen |

---

## 6. Sample Meal Plans

### Training Day (~2,600 kcal — Phase 1)

| Meal | Time | Food | P | C | F | kcal |
|------|------|------|---|---|---|------|
| **Meal 1 — Breakfast** | 7:00 AM | 3 whole eggs scrambled, 1 slice sourdough toast, 1 banana | 25 g | 50 g | 18 g | 460 |
| **Meal 2 — Pre-Training** | 11:00 AM | 150 g chicken breast, 200 g white rice (cooked), mixed greens, 1 tsp olive oil | 45 g | 70 g | 12 g | 570 |
| **Pre-Training Snack** | 12:30 PM | 1 banana + 1 tsp honey | 1 g | 35 g | 0 g | 145 |
| **Intra-Training** | 1:00-3:00 PM | 500 ml sports drink | 0 g | 30 g | 0 g | 120 |
| **Meal 3 — Post-Training** | 3:30 PM | 40 g whey protein, 80 g oats (dry weight), 150 g blueberries | 40 g | 85 g | 8 g | 570 |
| **Meal 4 — Dinner** | 7:00 PM | 180 g salmon fillet, 150 g sweet potato, 150 g broccoli | 42 g | 35 g | 18 g | 470 |
| **Meal 5 — Pre-Bed** | 9:30 PM | 200 g Greek yogurt (full fat), 20 g walnuts | 18 g | 15 g | 16 g | 275 |
| **DAILY TOTAL** | | | **171 g** | **320 g** | **72 g** | **~2,610** |

*Increase chicken portion at Meal 2 by 30 g to push protein closer to 185 g target. Adjust rice portion down by 25 g if weight loss stalls.*

### Rest Day (~2,100 kcal — Phase 1)

| Meal | Time | Food | P | C | F | kcal |
|------|------|------|---|---|---|------|
| **Meal 1 — Breakfast** | 8:30 AM | 3 whole eggs + 2 egg whites scrambled, 100 g cherry tomatoes, 1/4 avocado | 32 g | 10 g | 20 g | 350 |
| **Meal 2 — Lunch** | 12:30 PM | 180 g lean ground beef (93%), 120 g white rice (cooked), roasted vegetables | 45 g | 40 g | 15 g | 475 |
| **Meal 3 — Afternoon** | 4:00 PM | 30 g whey protein, 1 apple, 20 g almonds | 30 g | 30 g | 12 g | 350 |
| **Meal 4 — Dinner** | 7:00 PM | 150 g chicken thigh (skin removed), large mixed salad, 1 tbsp olive oil dressing | 38 g | 12 g | 18 g | 370 |
| **Meal 5 — Pre-Bed** | 9:30 PM | 250 g cottage cheese, 10 g honey | 32 g | 15 g | 5 g | 235 |
| **DAILY TOTAL** | | | **177 g** | **107 g** | **70 g** | **~1,780** |

*Add a rice cake or piece of fruit at lunch to bring closer to 2,100 kcal target if needed. Adjust carbs upward with simple additions, not fats.*

---

## 7. Hydration Protocol

### Daily Baseline (Non-Fight-Week)

| Metric | Calculation | Target |
|--------|-------------|--------|
| Body weight method | Current weight x 35-45 ml/kg | **3.0-3.8 L per day** |

Aim for the higher end on training days and in warm conditions.

### Around Training

| Window | Protocol |
|--------|----------|
| **Pre-training** (2 hours before) | 500 ml water, steadily sipped |
| **During training** | 600-1,200 ml per hour with electrolytes |
| **Post-training** | Replace 150% of fluid lost (weigh before/after if possible) |

### Electrolyte Targets During Training

- **Sodium:** 500-700 mg per liter of fluid
- **Potassium:** 150-200 mg per liter
- Add a pinch of salt to water or use a commercial electrolyte mix

### Monitoring

- **Urine color:** Aim for pale straw yellow. Clear = overhydrated. Dark yellow/amber = dehydrated.
- Check first thing in the morning — this is your best baseline indicator.
- If weigh-in shows > 2% body weight loss post-session, increase fluid intake next session.

---

## 8. Water Loading Protocol (Fight Week — Week 9)

> **WARNING: Water manipulation is DANGEROUS if done incorrectly. Work with an experienced coach or nutritionist who has managed weight cuts before. The protocol below is an outline only — individual responses vary significantly.**

**Pre-conditions:** You must be at or below 79 kg entering fight week. If you are above 80 kg at the start of Week 9, reconsider whether the water cut is safe — you may need to cut more than 3 kg acutely, which increases risk.

| Days Out from Weigh-In | Water Intake | Sodium | Carbs | Food | Notes |
|-------------------------|-------------|--------|-------|------|-------|
| **7 days (Saturday)** | 8 L/day | Normal (~3,000 mg) | Normal | Normal meals | Begin water loading — train kidneys to excrete at high rate |
| **6 days (Sunday)** | 8 L/day | Normal | Normal | Normal meals | Continue loading. Spread intake across waking hours. |
| **5 days (Monday)** | 8 L/day | Normal | Normal | Normal meals | Continue loading. Monitor urine — should be very clear. |
| **4 days (Tuesday)** | 6 L/day | Reduce by 50% (~1,500 mg) | Normal | Normal meals | Begin tapering. Body still excreting at high rate. |
| **3 days (Wednesday)** | 4 L/day | Reduce to minimal (~500 mg) | Begin reducing (~150 g) | Smaller meals, lean protein + low-fiber carbs | Taper continues. Avoid high-sodium foods. |
| **2 days (Thursday)** | 2 L/day | Minimal (~300 mg) | Low (~75 g) | Small meals: chicken, rice, banana | Body still excreting at high rate but intake is low. Weight drops noticeably. |
| **1 day (Friday — 24h before weigh-in)** | Sips only (~500 ml max) | Minimal | Very low (~50 g) | Light: chicken breast, small portion rice | Restrict to small sips as needed. Hot bath (20 min at 40-42C) for final 0.5-1 kg if needed. |
| **Weigh-in morning (Saturday)** | Nothing until after weigh-in | — | — | Nothing | Weigh in. Then immediately begin rehydration protocol below. |

**Additional fight week tactics:**
- Low-fiber diet from Wednesday onward to reduce gut content weight (~0.5 kg)
- A hot Epsom salt bath (20 min) the night before weigh-in can shed an extra 0.5-1 kg through sweat
- Sauna use should be limited to 15-20 minute sessions maximum with monitoring — never alone
- Weigh yourself multiple times per day during fight week to track progress

---

## 9. Rehydration & Refueling Plan (Post-Weigh-In)

Begin immediately after stepping off the scale. The goal is to regain as much of the cut weight as possible before fight time.

| Priority | Protocol | Timing |
|----------|----------|--------|
| **Fluid** | 1.5 L per kg of bodyweight lost. Sip steadily — do not chug. | Begin immediately, spread over 2-4 hours |
| **Electrolytes** | Mandatory with every drink. Sodium, potassium, magnesium. Use oral rehydration solution (ORS) or electrolyte mix. | With every fluid intake |
| **Carbohydrates** | 8-10 g/kg over the rehydration period. Simple carbs first (white rice, bananas, sports drinks), then gradually add complexity. | Begin within 30 min of weigh-in |
| **Protein** | 1.5-2 g/kg spread across meals. Lean sources (chicken, whey). | With each meal |
| **Fat** | Keep low initially — fats slow gastric emptying and can cause GI distress. Add moderate fat only in final pre-fight meal. | Minimal for first 4 hours, moderate after |

### Sample Post-Weigh-In Rehydration Schedule

| Time After Weigh-In | Action |
|---------------------|--------|
| 0-30 min | 500 ml ORS + banana + 30 g whey protein shake |
| 30-60 min | Sip 500 ml sports drink. Small bowl of white rice + chicken (200 g rice, 100 g chicken). |
| 1-2 hours | 500 ml water with electrolytes. Rice cakes with honey. |
| 2-4 hours | Full meal: 250 g white rice, 150 g chicken, steamed vegetables, small amount of butter. Continue sipping fluids. |
| 4-6 hours | Continue sipping fluids. Light snack if hungry: banana, yogurt, crackers. |
| Pre-fight meal (3-4 hours before fight) | Familiar, moderate meal: rice + chicken + banana. Nothing new or heavy. Small amount of caffeine if part of routine. |

> **Timing is critical.** You need a minimum of 12-24 hours between weigh-in and fight time to rehydrate properly. Same-day weigh-ins are significantly more dangerous — the rehydration window is too short to fully recover.

---

## 10. Supplement Stack

All supplements listed are **WADA-compliant as of the writing date.** Verify at [globaldro.com](https://www.globaldro.com) before taking any supplement.

| Supplement | Dose | Timing | Evidence | Key Benefit | Cut-Phase Notes |
|------------|------|--------|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Creatine Monohydrate** | 5 g/day | Any time (consistency matters) | A-tier | Strength, power output, recovery | **Discontinue 3 weeks before weigh-in** (Week 6) — creatine holds 1-2 kg intracellular water. Resume post-weigh-in. |
| **Caffeine** | 3-6 mg/kg (250-500 mg) | 45-60 min pre-training | A-tier | Alertness, power, endurance | Helpful during deficit for maintaining training intensity. Avoid after 2 PM. Consider 7-day washout before fight for maximal competition effect. |
| **Whey Protein** | 30-40 g per serving | Post-training or any meal gap | A-tier | Convenient complete protein | Essential during a cut to hit 185 g protein target. Not a replacement for whole food. |
| **Electrolytes** | 500-700 mg sodium/L | During training > 60 min | A-tier | Prevent cramping, maintain output | Even more important during a cut. Monitor sodium carefully during fight week water manipulation. |
| **Vitamin D3** | 3,000-5,000 IU/day | With a fat-containing meal | A-tier | Immune function, bone health, recovery | Caloric deficit can suppress immune function — vitamin D provides protective support. |
| **Magnesium Glycinate** | 400-500 mg/day | Split AM/PM; prioritize before bed | B-tier | Sleep quality, muscle relaxation, recovery | Sleep quality often suffers during a cut. Magnesium helps. Glycinate form is best absorbed. |
| **Omega-3 (EPA/DHA)** | 2-3 g combined EPA+DHA/day | With meals (split across 2 meals) | A-tier | Anti-inflammatory, joint health, brain function | Anti-inflammatory effect helpful when training volume is high and recovery is compromised by deficit. |
| **Beta-Alanine** | 3-6 g/day | Split into 2-3 doses | B-tier | Buffers lactic acid in 1-4 min efforts | Optional. Most useful for high-rep grappling rounds. Tingling (paresthesia) is harmless. |

---

## 11. Daily Weigh-In Protocol

Consistent, accurate daily weigh-ins are essential for tracking cut progress and making timely adjustments.

| Parameter | Protocol |
|-----------|----------|
| **Time** | Immediately upon waking |
| **Condition** | Fasted — no food, no water. After using the bathroom. |
| **Clothing** | Underwear only, or naked. Same every day. |
| **Scale** | Same digital scale every day. Place on a hard, flat surface (not carpet). |
| **Recording** | Log weight in a tracking app or spreadsheet immediately. Include the date. |
| **Trend tracking** | Use a 7-day rolling average, not single-day readings. Daily fluctuations of 0.5-1.5 kg are normal (water, food, bowel movements). |

### Decision Rules

| Scenario | Action |
|----------|--------|
| 7-day average is on target | Continue as planned |
| 7-day average is 0.5+ kg ahead of schedule | Slightly increase carbs on training days (+30 g) to protect performance |
| 7-day average is 0.5+ kg behind schedule | Add 20-30 min low-intensity cardio on 2-3 rest days. Do NOT increase deficit beyond 600 kcal. |
| 7-day average is 1+ kg behind schedule | Add cardio AND reduce rest day carbs by 30 g. Consult with coach/nutritionist. |
| Weight stalls for 10+ days | Likely water retention masking fat loss. Stay the course for 3-4 more days. If no change, reduce carbs by 20 g on rest days. |

---

## 12. WADA Compliance & Supplement Safety

### Verification Protocol

- **Before taking ANY supplement**, verify its status at [globaldro.com](https://www.globaldro.com) — the Global Drug Reference Online maintained by WADA-accredited agencies.
- Check every time you buy a new batch — even the same brand and product can change formulation between production runs.
- If a substance is not listed on GlobalDRO, do not assume it is permitted. Contact your sanctioning body for guidance.

### Recommended Certifications

| Certification | What It Means |
|--------------|---------------|
| **NSF Certified for Sport** | Independent testing for banned substances and label accuracy. Gold standard. |
| **Informed Sport** | Batch-tested by LGC for prohibited substances. Widely recognised internationally. |
| **BSCG Certified Drug Free** | Tests for 500+ drugs including WADA-prohibited substances. |

> **Use certified brands whenever possible.** The cost difference is minimal compared to the career-ending consequences of a positive test.

### Common Contamination Risks

| Product Category | Risk | Details |
|-----------------|------|---------|
| Pre-workouts | High | May contain undeclared stimulants (DMAA, DMHA, higenamine) that are prohibited in competition |
| Herbal supplements | High | May contain unlisted ingredients, heavy metals, or pro-hormones |
| Imported products | High | Products manufactured outside major regulated markets carry higher contamination risk |
| Fat burners / weight loss products | Very high | Frequently contain prohibited stimulants or diuretics |
| Protein powders (major brands) | Low | Generally safe, but verify certification |
| Creatine monohydrate (major brands) | Very low | One of the safest and most well-studied supplements |

### Athlete Responsibility

If competing under any athletic commission or sanctioning body, the athlete is **personally responsible for everything they ingest**. This is a strict liability, "no excuse" policy:

- "I didn't know it was in there" is not a valid defence.
- "My coach/nutritionist told me to take it" is not a valid defence.
- "The label didn't list it" is not a valid defence.

**You are responsible. Verify everything.**

---

## 13. Grocery List

### Proteins
- Chicken breast (boneless, skinless)
- Chicken thighs (skin-on for rest days)
- Salmon fillets
- Lean ground beef (93% lean)
- Whole eggs (free range)
- Cottage cheese
- Greek yogurt (full fat)
- Whey protein powder

### Carbohydrates
- White rice (jasmine or basmati)
- Oats (rolled or quick)
- Sweet potatoes
- White potatoes
- Sourdough bread
- Bananas (pre/post training staple)

### Fats
- Extra virgin olive oil
- Butter (small quantities)
- Avocados
- Almonds
- Walnuts
- Pumpkin seeds
- Natural peanut butter
- Dark chocolate (70%+ — limited during cut)

### Vegetables
- Broccoli
- Spinach / mixed greens
- Zucchini
- Bell peppers
- Cherry tomatoes
- Onions
- Garlic

### Fruits
- Bananas
- Blueberries
- Apples
- Lemons / limes

### Pantry Staples
- Honey
- Salt (sea salt or iodized)
- Black pepper
- Spice basics (paprika, cumin, garlic powder)
- Soy sauce or coconut aminos
- Rice vinegar
- Coffee (ground or beans)

### Supplements
- Creatine monohydrate (unflavored powder)
- Whey protein (chocolate or vanilla)
- Fish oil capsules (high EPA/DHA)
- Vitamin D3 softgels
- Magnesium glycinate capsules
- Electrolyte mix or sodium tablets
- Caffeine (coffee or capsules)
- Beta-alanine powder (optional)
- Oral rehydration solution (for fight week rehydration)
training-tools:
# Training Tools

> **Reference Guide** | Exercise library, warm-up protocols, cool-down sequences, and equipment lists.
> Use this section as a quick-reference during training sessions.

---

## Exercise Library

### Compound Barbell Lifts

| Exercise | Primary Muscles | Key Cues | MMA Transfer |
|----------|----------------|----------|--------------|
| Back Squat | Quads, glutes, adductors, erectors | Brace hard, spread the floor, hips and knees break together. Drive up through mid-foot. | Lower-body force for level changes, takedown defense, punching power from the ground up. |
| Front Squat | Quads, glutes, upper back, core | Elbows up the whole way. Sit straight down between hips. Drive up keeping torso vertical. | Upright torso pattern mimics clinch posture and double-leg finishes. Heavy anterior core demand. |
| Deadlift | Posterior chain, lats, grip | Push the floor away, lock lats, bar drags up the legs. Stand tall — squeeze glutes at lockout. | Total-body posterior chain for throws, slams, sprawls, and heavy rear-hand strikes. Grip endurance under load. |
| Romanian Deadlift (RDL) | Hamstrings, glutes, erectors | Push hips straight back. Bar stays on thighs/shins. Feel deep hamstring stretch, then drive hips forward. | Eccentric hamstring strength reduces knee-injury risk from sprawling and kicking. |
| Bench Press | Pecs, anterior delts, triceps | Control bar to lower chest, drive through feet, press bar in slight arc back over shoulders. | Horizontal pressing power for frames, stiff-arms, bench pressing opponent off from bottom. |
| Overhead Press | Deltoids, triceps, upper traps, core | Press bar straight up — move head out of the way, then back through. Lock out over mid-foot. | Overhead strength for posting on cage, hand fighting, resisting being driven down in clinch. |
| Barbell Row | Lats, rhomboids, rear delts, biceps | Pull bar to lower sternum, squeeze shoulder blades at top, control the negative. Torso stays still. | Pulling strength for clinch work, underhook control, arm drags, and guillotine finishes. |
| Hang Clean | Quads, glutes, traps, upper back | Explode hips, shrug, pull elbows high, catch in front rack with quarter squat. | Triple extension = same motor pattern as shooting a double-leg or throwing an uppercut. Rate of force development. |
| Push Press | Deltoids, triceps, quads, core | Quick dip keeping torso vertical, then drive aggressively through legs and press overhead. | Legs-to-arms force transfer — same kinetic chain used in every punch and clinch off-balancing. |

---

### Single-Leg / Unilateral

| Exercise | Primary Muscles | Key Cues | MMA Transfer |
|----------|----------------|----------|--------------|
| RFESS (Bulgarian Split Squat) | Quads, glutes, adductors | Lower straight down until rear knee nearly touches floor. Drive through front heel. Keep torso upright. | Single-leg strength for kicking, sprawling off one leg, maintaining base when opponent grabs a leg. |
| Single-Leg RDL | Hamstrings, glutes, core (anti-rotation) | Hinge at hip, free leg extends behind as counterbalance. Back stays flat. Squeeze glute to stand. | Standing leg stability for kicks, single-leg TD defense. Anti-rotation mirrors rotational chaos of grappling. |
| Walking Lunges | Quads, glutes, hip flexors | Take a long step forward, lower rear knee toward floor, drive through front heel. Stay tall. | Dynamic single-leg strength for shooting entries, level changes, maintaining balance in the pocket. |
| Step-Ups | Quads, glutes | Drive through top foot to stand on box. Do NOT push off bottom foot. Control descent. | Concentric single-leg power for standing up from bottom, penetration steps for takedowns. |
| Pistol Squat (Progression) | Quads, glutes, hip flexors, ankles | Sit back and down on one leg, keeping heel flat. Use band or box assist as needed. | Ultimate single-leg strength/mobility for recovering balance after sweeps, deep scramble positions. |

---

### Power / Explosive

| Exercise | Primary Muscles | Key Cues | MMA Transfer |
|----------|----------------|----------|--------------|
| Box Jumps | Quads, glutes, calves | Swing arms, extend hips-knees-ankles, land softly on box with full foot contact. Step down. | Rate of force development for explosive takedown entries, sprawls, and closing distance. |
| Broad Jumps | Glutes, quads, hamstrings | Swing arms forward, drive hips and jump for max distance. Absorb landing with soft knees. | Horizontal explosive power — exact vector for shooting takedowns and closing distance for strikes. |
| Med Ball Overhead Slam | Lats, core, shoulders | Brace core, slam ball into ground as hard as possible using whole body. Hinge and follow through. | Full-body power expression similar to hammerfists from top or slam finishes. |
| Med Ball Rotational Slam | Obliques, core, hips | Rotate hips first, then trunk, then arms — slam ball into wall. Load from back hip. | Directly trains rotational power sequence of hooks, overhands, and round kicks. |
| Med Ball Chest Pass | Pecs, triceps, core | Drive ball off chest explosively into wall. Catch and immediately redirect. Stay on balls of feet. | Horizontal pushing speed for frames, posting, bench-pressing opponent off you. |
| KB Swings | Glutes, hamstrings, core | Snap hips forward — arms just go along for the ride. Squeeze glutes hard at top. | Hip snap transfers to striking power, sprawl speed, and bridging/shrimping. High-rep = posterior chain endurance. |
| KB Snatch | Glutes, hamstrings, shoulders | Hike back, snap hips, guide bell around wrist and punch through to lockout overhead. | Combines explosive hip extension with overhead stability and grip. Full-body power expression. |
| Battle Rope Slams | Shoulders, core, lats, grip | Alternate arms or slam both together. Generate force from hips. Keep a rhythm. | Upper-body power-endurance under fatigue — simulates repeated explosive arm actions of punching in later rounds. |

---

### Core / Anti-Movement

| Exercise | Primary Muscles | Key Cues | MMA Transfer |
|----------|----------------|----------|--------------|
| Pallof Press | Obliques, transverse abdominis | Press handle straight out from chest and hold. Resist rotation. Keep hips square. | Anti-rotation core stability — resisting being twisted in clinch, scrambles, and when absorbing body shots. |
| Ab Wheel Rollout | Rectus abdominis, obliques, lats | Roll forward slowly, keeping hips extended. Go as far as you can control, then pull back with abs. | Anti-extension strength for maintaining posture in clinch, resisting back bends in wrestling. |
| Hanging Leg Raises | Hip flexors, lower abs, grip | Raise legs to parallel (or toes to bar). Control descent — no swinging. Initiate from core. | Hip flexor strength for guard retention, high kicks, knee strikes. Grip endurance while working core. |
| Copenhagen Plank | Adductors, obliques | Top foot on bench, lift hips and bottom leg to form straight line. Hold. | Adductor and lateral core strength for closed guard, body locks, and groin injury prevention. |
| Dead Bug | Deep core stabilizers | Extend opposite arm and leg toward floor while keeping low back glued to mat. | Core stabilizing spine while limbs move independently — exactly what happens during transitions. |
| Farmer's Carry | Traps, grip, core (anti-lateral flexion) | Walk with short, controlled steps. Stay tall — don't lean. Squeeze handles hard. | Total-body stability, grip endurance, and postural strength. Directly transfers to clinch endurance. |
| Suitcase Carry | Obliques, core, grip | Walk resisting the urge to lean toward loaded side. Stay perfectly upright. | Unilateral anti-lateral flexion — resisting being pulled to one side in clinch, defending arm drags. |
| Turkish Get-Up | Full body | Roll to elbow, post on hand, bridge hips, sweep leg under to kneeling, stand up. Eyes on bell. | Most MMA-specific core exercise. Every phase mimics a grappling transition. |

---

### Upper Body Accessories

| Exercise | Key Cues | MMA Transfer |
|----------|----------|--------------|
| Weighted Pull-Ups | Initiate with lats, pull chin over bar, control descent to full extension. | Pulling strength for clinch control, guillotines, arm drags. Grip under load. |
| Dumbbell Row | Pull dumbbell to hip, squeezing shoulder blade back and down. | Unilateral pulling for single-collar ties, underhook battles. |
| Landmine Press | Press bar up and forward at slight angle. Squeeze core to prevent rotation. | Angled pressing mimics jab/cross. Anti-rotation demand mirrors posting. |
| Incline DB Press | Press dumbbells up and slightly inward. Lower to shoulders with control. | Upper chest pressing for frames from bottom, posting on cage. |
| Push-Up Variations | Standard, Diamond, Archer, Clap — all with full ROM and proper alignment. | Pressing endurance, explosive frames, short-range pushing power. |
| Dips | Lower until upper arms parallel to floor, press to full lockout. | Pressing from depressed shoulder position — posting on mat, pressing up from bottom. |
| Face Pulls | Pull rope to face, split hands apart and externally rotate. Squeeze for a beat. | Shoulder health and postural balance against high volumes of punching. |
| Band Pull-Aparts | Pull band apart by squeezing shoulder blades together. Slow return. | Scapular health and posture maintenance. Daily prehab movement. |

---

### Grip & Neck

| Exercise | Key Cues | MMA Transfer |
|----------|----------|--------------|
| Plate Pinch Holds | Squeeze two plates together (smooth sides out). Hold for time. | Pinch grip for controlling wrists, maintaining grip on sweaty opponent. |
| Towel Pull-Ups | Perform pull-ups while gripping towels draped over bar. | Grip endurance on thick, pliable surface — mimics controlling sweaty wrist/ankle. |
| Fat Grip Rows | Row with thick handles. Reduce weight 20-30% vs normal. | Open-hand grip strength for no-gi grappling and wrist control. |
| Neck Harness (4-Way) | Controlled flexion/extension through comfortable ROM. 3-4 sec per rep. NEVER do neck bridges. | Strong neck directly reduces concussion risk. Resists guillotines, can openers, cranks. |
| Band Neck Isometrics | Hold head neutral against band pull. 10-20 sec holds, all 4 directions. | Low-impact daily neck strengthening. Isometric endurance for real-time resistance. |

---

### Conditioning Modalities

| Exercise | Key Cues | MMA Transfer |
|----------|----------|--------------|
| Assault Bike Intervals | Push/pull with arms, drive with legs. All-out for work, easy spin for rest. | Whole-body energy system training. Explosive-burst-then-recover pattern of MMA rounds. |
| Rowing — 500m Repeats | Drive with legs first, then hips, then arms. Target consistent splits. | Full posterior chain conditioning. Lactic tolerance for scrambles and late takedown battles. |
| Jump Rope | Small bounces, turn from wrists. Stay relaxed. | Footwork coordination, calf endurance, rhythm — foundation of striking movement. |
| Sled Push / Pull | Push: drive with legs, stay low. Pull: sit back and row hand-over-hand. | Zero eccentric = heavy conditioning without DOMS. Pushing mimics cage driving, pulling mimics dragging. |
| Shadow Boxing | Throw combinations with intent. Move feet. Visualize an opponent. | Most specific conditioning for striking. Technical practice under fatigue. |
| Bag Work | Throw real combinations. Move head and feet between combos. Hit through the bag. | Full-contact striking conditioning. Builds punch endurance and timing under fatigue. |
| Sprawl-to-Burpee | Drop into sprawl (hips to floor), pop back to feet. Reset stance and repeat. | Most MMA-specific bodyweight conditioning. Combines TD defense with explosive recovery. |

---

### Prehab / Mobility

| Exercise | Key Cues | Purpose |
|----------|----------|---------|
| Band External Rotations | Rotate forearm outward, elbow glued to ribs. Slow both directions. | Rotator cuff health — non-negotiable for fighters. |
| 90/90 Hip Switches | Rotate both legs to opposite position in one smooth motion. Chest tall. | Hip IR/ER mobility for guard play, sweeps, hip escapes, kicking. |
| Hip CARs | Largest possible circle with knee — slowly, with maximum tension. | Maintaining full hip ROM. Prevents loss from tight guards and heavy squatting. |
| Thoracic Spine Rotations | Side-lying, rotate top arm up opening chest to ceiling. Knees glued together. | T-spine rotation is the engine of punching power and shot defense. |
| Goblet Squat | Sit between hips, elbows push knees out. Chest tall. | Warm-up for squat patterns. Upright position transfers to clinch posture. |
| World's Greatest Stretch | Lunge → rotate inside arm up → straighten front leg for hamstring stretch. | Most efficient dynamic warm-up. Addresses hip flexors, T-spine, hamstrings, groin. |
| Band Dislocates | Keeping arms straight, raise band overhead and behind back in slow arc. | Full shoulder ROM warm-up. Counteracts tight anterior shoulders from punching. |

---

## Warm-Up Protocols

### Protocol 1: Upper Body Strength (~10 min)

| Order | Exercise | Duration/Reps | Cue |
|-------|----------|---------------|-----|
| 1 | Jumping jacks | 30 sec | Light and rhythmic, get blood flowing |
| 2 | Arm circles (forward + backward) | 15 sec each direction | Full range, gradually increase size |
| 3 | Shoulder CARs | 5 each direction per arm | Slow, controlled, maximal range |
| 4 | Band pull-aparts | 2 x 15 | Squeeze shoulder blades together |
| 5 | Push-up walkouts (inchworms) | 5 reps | Slow walk out to full plank, brief hold |
| 6 | Thoracic rotations (quadruped) | 5 each side | Rotate to open chest to ceiling |
| 7 | Ramp-up set 1 (main lift) | 8 reps @ empty bar | Focus on bar path and setup |
| 8 | Ramp-up set 2 (main lift) | 5 reps @ ~50% working weight | Lock in cues |

---

### Protocol 2: Lower Body Strength (~12 min)

| Order | Exercise | Duration/Reps | Cue |
|-------|----------|---------------|-----|
| 1 | Light jog in place / high knees | 30 sec | Easy pace, wake up the legs |
| 2 | Hip CARs (standing) | 5 each direction per leg | Slow circles, keep pelvis stable |
| 3 | 90/90 switches | 8 total (4 each side) | Sit tall, rotate hips smoothly |
| 4 | Ankle mobility (knee-over-toe rocks) | 10 each side | Heel stays down, drive knee past toes |
| 5 | Bodyweight squats | 10 reps | Full depth, chest up, knees out |
| 6 | Walking lunges | 8 each leg | Long stride, upright torso |
| 7 | Banded lateral walks | 10 each direction | Stay low in half squat, push knees out |
| 8 | Fire hydrants | 10 each side | Quadruped, core braced, lift knee to side |
| 9 | Ramp-up set 1 (main lift) | 8 reps @ empty bar | Groove the pattern |
| 10 | Ramp-up set 2 (main lift) | 5 reps @ ~50% working weight | Controlled descent, strong drive |

### Protocol 3: Power Day (~11 min)

| 6 | Hip CARs | 3 each direction per leg | Open up hips |

| 8 | Box jump build-up (low box) | 3 reps | Soft landing, step down |
| 9 | Box jump build-up (working height) | 3 reps | Stick the landing, quiet feet |
| 10 | Ramp-up set (main power lift) | 3 reps @ ~50% | Fast and crisp |

### Protocol 4: MMA Striking (~10 min)

| 1 | Jump rope | 3 min | Stay on balls of feet, light bounce |

| 8 | Shadow boxing — add hooks, uppercuts | 1 min | Stay relaxed, snap punches |
| 9 | Shadow boxing — full combos + kicks | 1 min | Progressive intensity, move feet |

**Add Pre-Session Neck Protocol:** 4-way isometric holds, 10 sec × 2 rounds each direction (~3 min)

### Protocol 5: MMA Grappling (~12 min)
